claimed_contribution: The paper develops a framework to quantify fashion choices by extracting compatibility and distinctiveness from images with deep learning and embedding these features in a dynamic structural choice model.
It provides a first systematic micro-level empirical analysis of how fast-fashion copycats affect high-end brands in a social media setting with consumer-level posting behavior.

It identifies a novel market expansion effect of copycats, with one static mechanism (mix-and-match affordability) and two dynamic mechanisms (value enhancement via popularity and cost reduction via sponsorship opportunities), and uses these to evaluate copyright-policy counterfactuals.
context: The empirical setting is lookbook.nu, a fashion-specific social media platform where users post outfit photos, tag item brands, and receive likes from peers. The study focuses on how fast-fashion, designer, and mega couture items are chosen and combined in users' posted looks.
design_and_data: The authors collect platform history from August 2013 to August 2017 for a random sample of 10,262 registered users (9,915 active users), yielding 64,681 fashion posts with images, brand tags, timestamps, and likes. They extract clothing and user-appearance features from images using computer vision and deep learning, including compatibility, distinctiveness, facial attractiveness, BMI, and predicted gender. They estimate a forward-looking dynamic structural model of posting, brand choice, and style choice with user heterogeneity in a hierarchical Bayesian framework, with weekly periods and popularity measured by cumulative likes. They run counterfactual copyright-ban simulations using similarity thresholds informed by external original-copycat image pairs from Fashion Copycats and Diet Prada.
main_findings: Fast-fashion copycats have both a negative cannibalization effect and a positive market expansion effect on high-end brands in social media posting choices.

The market expansion effect operates through three mechanisms: a static mix-and-match affordability channel and two dynamic channels in which popularity raises posting value and lowers future costs (e.g., sponsorship-related cost reduction).

Counterfactuals show threshold-dependent policy effects: banning only extremely similar copycats increases mega couture posting, but banning moderately similar copycats decreases posting overall and reduces mega couture posting.
one_line_summary: Using lookbook.nu data and a dynamic structural model with deep-learning image features, the paper shows that fast-fashion copycats can both hurt and help high-end brand popularity on social media, with policy outcomes depending on how strictly similarity is regulated.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior copycat research by estimating micro-level consumer brand-style choices in fashion social media, rather than relying only on predefined simulation mechanisms or aggregate outcomes. It introduces and empirically separates a market expansion effect that the authors position as distinct from the promotional effect in counterfeit and piracy studies.
research_gap: The paper argues that the effect of fashion copycats on high-end brands is empirically unclear. It states that progress has been limited because fashion is hard to quantify and consumer-level data on fashion choices have been scarce.
research_question: How do fast-fashion copycats affect the popularity of high-end fashion brands in a social media environment? The paper asks whether copycats mainly cannibalize high-end brands or can also expand their popularity, through which static and dynamic mechanisms, and how bans at different copycat-similarity thresholds would change outcomes.
